refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved, distance, cycles) over the evaluation period, and that these data can be aggregated and reported to stakeholders in a format that allows calculation of energy_per_ton. Confirm the measurement system has documented accuracy and traceability.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall aggregate and report energy consumption and work output data to authorized stakeholders.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and data aggregation layer.
DP2: Embed energy sensors in prime mover; embed load sensors on transport mechanism; fuse signals in real-time controller; compute and log energy_per_ton locally with periodic reporting.

DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs (manual or automated pump records) and work output via independent weighbridge or load cell array; reconcile via post-shift batch calculation.

DP4: Deploy distributed sensor network (energy, load, position) with edge compute nodes; aggregate via message broker; compute energy_per_ton in centralized analytics service with configurable evaluation windows.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and data aggregation layer.

potential_sources: Energy metering and work output metering remain logically independent; failure in one does not corrupt the other.
Asynchronous computation decouples sensor acquisition from ratio calculation, allowing independent evolution of measurement hardware and analytics logic.
Data aggregation layer introduces a single point of dependency; mitigate via redundancy or fallback to manual reconciliation.
Evaluation period definition and acceptance criteria thresholds are external policy inputs, not embedded in DP logic.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low functional coupling because energy and work measurement are orthogonal concerns. The asynchronous aggregation pattern avoids tight real-time coupling. DP2 exhibits higher coupling (real-time fusion in controller); DP3 introduces manual process coupling (labor-dependent accuracy); DP4 introduces architectural coupling via message broker and edge compute dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel or grid) is metered at a single, accessible point. Risk: Distributed or mobile energy sources (e.g., multiple fuel cans, on-site generation) may require multi-point instrumentation, increasing complexity.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage) is measurable at a single delivery point. Risk: If material is distributed across multiple drop-off locations or partial loads are common, aggregation logic must handle fragmented records.

Assumption: Measurement devices (energy meter, load cell) have sufficient accuracy and calibration stability over the evaluation period. Risk: Drift or miscalibration introduces systematic bias in energy_per_ton ratio; requires periodic verification protocol.
Assumption: Data aggregation layer has sufficient uptime and data retention. Risk: Loss of intermediate records prevents post-hoc reconciliation; requires backup logging strategy.

Assumption: Evaluation period is well-defined and operationally stable (no major equipment changes, operational mode shifts). Risk: If equipment is swapped or operational parameters change mid-period, ratio becomes non-comparable; requires stratification or re-baselining.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not conflict with sensor placement or data collection. Risk: Safety constraints may restrict sensor access or require additional guarding, increasing cost and complexity.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output, such that energy_per_ton = (total energy input measured at source) / (total work output measured at delivery point) is computed asynchronously and reconciled over a defined evaluation period, with measurement accuracy sufficient to detect ≤ E_target compliance within ±5% confidence interval, and with data retention and fallback procedures to support post-hoc audit and manual reconciliation if primary metering fails.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy efficiency metering and data aggregation layer that instruments fuel/electricity input at source, work output at delivery point, and computes energy ratio asynchronously across the fleet.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw energy consumption data (fuel/electricity) from Vehicle and work output metrics (tonnage, distance) from point-of-delivery sensors.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Publishes computed energy efficiency ratios and aggregated performance analytics back to Vehicle and Teleoperation for visibility.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Asynchronous data transport for metering streams and aggregation results; decouples real-time vehicle operations from batch efficiency computation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest cohesion: NGHE Solution is explicitly a fleet-level optimization and analytics platform. Independent metering, asynchronous aggregation, and ratio computation are core to its domain. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (in/out) and Network Link needed; no tight coupling to Vehicle control logic or Teleoperation UI.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a single-unit operational entity. Placing fleet-wide energy aggregation and asynchronous ratio computation here violates separation of concerns. Would require Vehicle to maintain state across multiple units and manage network-deferred analytics—high diffusion to Teleoperation and NGHE Solution.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command layer. Energy metering and asynchronous aggregation are not UI concerns. Placing this DP here couples analytics logic to operator interaction, creating tight diffusion to Vehicle, NGHE Solution, and Safety Services.

Very poor fit: Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection and emergency response. Energy efficiency metering is orthogonal to safety. Placing this DP here introduces unrelated state and computation, with high diffusion to all other modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally a fleet-level analytics capability: independent instrumentation of energy input and work output, asynchronous aggregation, and ratio computation. This is a core responsibility of the NGHE (Next-Generation Haulage Efficiency) Solution platform, which is designed to optimize fleet performance across multiple vehicles. The asynchronous, decoupled nature of the DP aligns with NGHE's role as a backend analytics and optimization service, not a real-time vehicle control or safety function.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation must reliably stream raw metering data (fuel, electricity, tonnage) to NGHE Solution via Telemetry and Network Link. If data transport is lossy or delayed, energy ratio computation becomes inaccurate. Mitigation: implement robust buffering and retry logic in Network Link; define SLA for Telemetry data freshness.

Risk: NGHE Solution must maintain synchronized time across distributed metering points (source and delivery). Clock skew could corrupt energy ratio calculations. Mitigation: enforce NTP or GPS-based time synchronization across all metering endpoints.

Risk: Asynchronous aggregation introduces latency between energy input and ratio output. If Teleoperation or Vehicle requires real-time efficiency feedback for immediate optimization, this DP may not satisfy that need. Mitigation: clarify whether efficiency ratio is for post-hoc analysis (acceptable latency) or real-time control (requires synchronous computation in Vehicle).

Risk: NGHE Solution becomes a single point of failure for fleet efficiency visibility. If NGHE Solution is unavailable, metering and aggregation halt. Mitigation: implement redundancy and failover for NGHE Solution; ensure Vehicle can operate independently if NGHE is offline.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution path: (1) instrument energy input at source, (2) instrument work output at delivery point, (3) compute ratio asynchronously via independent metering and aggregation. The DP is logically sound and measurable against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Data transport reliability: Vehicle and point-of-delivery sensors must reliably stream raw metering data (fuel, electricity, tonnage) to NGHE Solution via Telemetry and Network Link. Lossy or delayed transport will corrupt energy ratio computation. Mitigation: implement robust buffering, retry logic, and define SLA for Telemetry data freshness.

Distributed time synchronization: NGHE Solution must maintain synchronized time across metering points (source and delivery). Clock skew will corrupt energy ratio calculations. Mitigation: enforce NTP or GPS-based time synchronization across all metering endpoints.

Latency vs. real-time feedback: Asynchronous aggregation introduces latency between energy input and ratio output. If Teleoperation or Vehicle requires real-time efficiency feedback for immediate operational optimization, this DP may not satisfy that need. Clarify whether efficiency ratio is for post-hoc analysis (acceptable latency) or real-time control (requires synchronous computation in Vehicle).

Single point of failure: NGHE Solution becomes a critical dependency for fleet efficiency visibility. If NGHE Solution is unavailable, metering and aggregation halt, and efficiency visibility is lost. Mitigation: implement redundancy and failover for NGHE Solution; ensure Vehicle can operate independently if NGHE is offline.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership candidate with a containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08. The DP is fundamentally a fleet-level analytics capability (independent instrumentation, asynchronous aggregation, ratio computation), which aligns with NGHE's role as a backend optimization service. The asynchronous, decoupled architecture minimizes coupling to Vehicle control logic and Teleoperation UI. External interfaces (Telemetry in/out, Network Link) are minimal and well-defined. No evidence of DP scatter across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Containment hypothesis is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution outputs. DP1 specifies instrumentation points (source and delivery) and computation method (asynchronous ratio), enabling clear traceability from FR to IM. Verification requires: (1) validation that metering instruments are calibrated and accurate, (2) confirmation that aggregation logic correctly computes energy_per_ton, (3) confirmation that computed ratio is reported back to Teleoperation for visibility. All are feasible within NGHE Solution boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns metering instrumentation logic, data aggregation, and ratio computation. Vehicle and point-of-delivery sensors are data sources (external to NGHE Solution). Teleoperation is a consumer of efficiency reports. Network Link provides transport. Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping. No ambiguity in responsibility allocation.

recommended_revisions: Clarify latency tolerance: Specify whether efficiency ratio is required for post-hoc analysis (batch, acceptable latency of hours/days) or real-time operational feedback (requires synchronous computation in Vehicle or edge layer). Current DP assumes asynchronous batch processing; if real-time feedback is required, DP must be revised to include synchronous computation at Vehicle or edge.

Define data freshness SLA: Specify maximum acceptable latency for Telemetry data transport (e.g., 'raw metering data must reach NGHE Solution within 5 minutes of generation'). This ensures energy ratio computation is based on timely data and supports acceptance criterion verification.

Specify time synchronization mechanism: Explicitly require NTP or GPS-based time synchronization across all metering endpoints (source, delivery, NGHE Solution) to prevent clock skew corruption of energy ratio calculations.

Add redundancy and failover strategy: Specify how NGHE Solution will be made resilient (e.g., active-passive failover, multi-region deployment) and define fallback behavior if NGHE Solution is unavailable (e.g., Vehicle continues operation; efficiency visibility is degraded but not critical).

Define metering instrument accuracy and calibration: Specify accuracy requirements for fuel/electricity meters and tonnage sensors (e.g., ±2% accuracy) and calibration frequency to ensure energy_per_ton computation meets acceptance criterion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, containment is strong (0.92), and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions expose critical design dependencies that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs to ensure safety, reliability, and operational correctness. Specifically: (1) latency tolerance is unspecified and affects real-time vs. batch processing trade-offs; (2) data freshness SLA is missing, creating risk of stale metering data; (3) time synchronization is assumed but not explicitly required; (4) NGHE Solution resilience is not architected; (5) metering instrument accuracy is not specified. These are not minor clarifications—they are architectural decisions that must be elevated to sub-FR level to ensure the parent FR (Measure and report energy efficiency ratio) is fully decomposed and traceable. High safety criticality reinforces the need for explicit sub-FRs to address reliability, redundancy, and failure modes.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Ensure metering data freshness and timeliness (raw metering data from source and delivery points must reach NGHE Solution within defined SLA to support energy ratio computation)
FR1.2: Maintain synchronized time across all metering endpoints (source, delivery, NGHE Solution) to prevent clock skew corruption of energy ratio calculations

FR1.3: Guarantee NGHE Solution availability and resilience (NGHE Solution must remain operational or gracefully degrade to ensure continuous or near-continuous efficiency visibility and prevent single point of failure)

FR1.4: Ensure metering instrument accuracy and calibration (fuel, electricity, and tonnage sensors must meet specified accuracy requirements and be calibrated at defined intervals to ensure energy_per_ton computation meets acceptance criterion)

FR1.5: Clarify and satisfy latency requirements for efficiency ratio feedback (efficiency ratio must be computed and delivered within acceptable latency bounds to support intended use case: post-hoc analysis vs. real-time operational feedback)

revise.why: DP1 is logically sound but under-specifies critical design decisions. Revise DP1 to explicitly reference sub-DP solutions for: (1) data freshness SLA enforcement (e.g., buffering, retry logic, timeout handling in Telemetry and Network Link); (2) time synchronization mechanism (e.g., NTP or GPS-based sync across metering endpoints); (3) NGHE Solution redundancy and failover strategy (e.g., active-passive, multi-region, or edge-based fallback); (4) metering instrument accuracy and calibration process (e.g., ±2% accuracy, quarterly calibration); (5) latency tolerance specification (e.g., batch processing with 24-hour acceptable latency vs. synchronous computation with <5-minute latency). These sub-DPs will be derived from the sub-FRs and will refine the parent DP into a complete, implementable design.

assumptions: Efficiency ratio is primarily used for post-hoc fleet analysis and optimization, not real-time vehicle control feedback (latency tolerance is hours/days, not seconds).
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity and network bandwidth to aggregate metering data from all vehicles in the fleet without bottleneck.
Vehicle and point-of-delivery sensors are already instrumented with fuel, electricity, and tonnage measurement capability; NGHE Solution does not need to add new hardware sensors.
Telemetry and Network Link infrastructure is available and can be configured to support data freshness SLA (e.g., 5-minute latency).
Time synchronization infrastructure (NTP or GPS) is available across all metering endpoints.
NGHE Solution can be deployed with redundancy (e.g., multi-region, active-passive failover) without prohibitive cost or complexity.
Metering instruments can be calibrated to ±2% accuracy or better and calibration can be performed at defined intervals (e.g., quarterly).
open_risks: Latency sensitivity: If real-time efficiency feedback is required for vehicle control or driver feedback, asynchronous batch processing in NGHE Solution is insufficient. Mitigation: clarify latency tolerance early; if real-time is required, revise DP to include synchronous computation at vehicle or edge layer.
Data transport reliability: Lossy or delayed Telemetry data will corrupt energy ratio computation. Mitigation: implement robust buffering, retry logic, and define and enforce data freshness SLA.
Clock skew: Distributed time synchronization failure will corrupt energy ratio calculations. Mitigation: enforce NTP or GPS-based time synchronization; monitor and alert on clock skew.

NGHE Solution single point of failure: If NGHE Solution is unavailable, efficiency visibility is lost. Mitigation: implement redundancy and failover; define fallback behavior (e.g., vehicle operates independently; efficiency visibility is degraded).

Metering instrument accuracy: If fuel, electricity, or tonnage sensors are inaccurate or uncalibrated, energy_per_ton computation will be incorrect. Mitigation: specify accuracy requirements (e.g., ±2%); implement calibration process and schedule; validate instruments before deployment.

Safety criticality: High safety criticality suggests that efficiency ratio may be used for operational decisions (e.g., route optimization, load balancing) that affect vehicle safety or cargo integrity. Mitigation: ensure energy ratio computation is auditable and traceable; implement data validation and anomaly detection; define acceptance criteria for ratio accuracy and freshness.
evidence_needed: Latency tolerance specification: Confirm whether efficiency ratio is required for post-hoc analysis (batch, acceptable latency of hours/days) or real-time operational feedback (requires synchronous computation in vehicle or edge layer). Document in revised DP1.

Data freshness SLA: Define maximum acceptable latency for Telemetry data transport (e.g., 'raw metering data must reach NGHE Solution within 5 minutes of generation'). Validate that Telemetry and Network Link infrastructure can meet this SLA.
Time synchronization mechanism: Specify NTP or GPS-based time synchronization requirement across all metering endpoints. Validate that infrastructure supports this requirement.

NGHE Solution redundancy and failover strategy: Design and document redundancy approach (e.g., active-passive failover, multi-region deployment). Define fallback behavior if NGHE Solution is unavailable.

Metering instrument accuracy and calibration: Specify accuracy requirements for fuel/electricity meters and tonnage sensors (e.g., ±2% accuracy). Define calibration frequency and process. Validate that existing instruments meet requirements or identify upgrade needs.

Acceptance criterion verification plan: Document how energy_per_ton ≤ E_target will be verified. Include validation of metering instrument accuracy, aggregation logic correctness, and ratio reporting to Teleoperation.
